project_overview.project_name: COVID-19 Emergency Response and Health Systems Preparedness Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To respond to, and mitigate the threat posed by COVID-19 and strengthen national systems for public health preparedness in Afghanistan
project_overview.total_financing: US$100.4 million (IDA Grant: US$81 million + Crisis Response Window: US$19.4 million)
project_overview.implementation_period: March 31, 2024 (Expected Closing Date); Approval Date: April 2, 2020
project_overview.beneficiaries: Infected people, at-risk populations, medical and emergency personnel, service providers (public and private), medical and testing facilities, national health agencies, staff of key technical departments and provincial health offices, nationwide coverage across all 34 provinces

primary_locations: Herat Province (high-risk border area with Iran)
Urban areas and major transport centers
Border provinces (particularly those bordering Iran and China)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Afghanistan is designated as a fragile state with ongoing conflict. Civilian casualties exceeded 10,000 in 2019. Conflict-induced internally displaced people (IDPs) increased from 369,700 in 2018 to over 400,000 in 2019. About 505,000 refugees returned in 2019. Service delivery model has proven resilient to conflict and insecurity, with services provided nationwide including in insecure areas where Government footprint is limited. Service providers face pressure from anti-government elements (AGEs) with demands for illicit payments.

key_components.description: Enhance disease detection through surveillance capacity building, laboratory and diagnostic system strengthening, sample collection and transportation standardization, point-of-care diagnostics, national accreditation for testing. Local containment through isolation units in hospitals, infection control training, protective equipment procurement, diagnostics and essential items. Comprehensive communication and behavior change interventions including community mobilization through CDCs, health shuras, religious and tribal leaders. Call center establishment for public inquiries and case screening.
key_components.financing: US$34 million (US$14 million from COVID-19 FTF + US$20 million from IDA)

key_components.description: Strengthen essential health care service delivery through preparedness planning, specialized units in selected hospitals, treatment guidelines, hospital infection control interventions, oxygen delivery systems, medicines, hazard pay and death benefits for health workers. Rehabilitation and equipment of selected health facilities, intra-hospital infection control measures, safe water and sanitation improvements, medical waste management strengthening, additional health personnel mobilization, training, medical supplies, diagnostic reagents, operational expenses, technical assistance. Management capacity building for rapid outbreak response, public health workforce development including epidemiologists, data managers, laboratory technicians, emergency management specialists.

Address negative externalities from widespread COVID-19 outbreak. Support social distancing measures and mitigate impacts on children's learning through remote learning options (radio broadcast, distance delivery). Personal hygiene promotion, handwashing, proper cooking, mask distribution and use. Mental health and psychosocial services for vulnerable communities. Comprehensive communication strategies.

key_components.component_name: Component 4: Implementation Management and Monitoring and Evaluation
key_components.description: Strengthen public structures for project coordination and management at central and local levels. Support financial management and procurement. Monitoring and evaluation of prevention and preparedness, capacity building for clinical and public health research, joint-learning across countries. Third-party monitoring of progress. Personal data collection and processing in accordance with best practices.

In the event of an Eligible Crisis or Emergency, the project will contribute to providing immediate and effective response. Approximately US$10 million IDA unallocated for reallocation across components as needed.
key_components.financing: US$0 million (contingent); US$10 million unallocated for reallocation

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Public Health (MOPH), Islamic Republic of Afghanistan. Deputy Minister for Policy and Planning serves as Project Coordinator with support from Sehatmandi Coordination Office (SCO). Project oversight through COVID-19 Emergency Response Committee. Multisectoral aspects guided by Presidential Multisectoral COVID-19 Response Committee chaired by H.E. the President/Vice President.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Health services delivered through contracted service providers (SPs) in 31 provinces under existing Sehatmandi arrangements. Three provinces (Parwan, Kapisa, Panjshir) managed directly by MOPH with technical assistance. Tertiary hospitals in Kabul managed by MOPH or contracted to NGO/UN Agency. Provincial hospitals and comprehensive health centers managed by contracted SPs. Coordination with Government (MOPH and MOF), UN agencies, WHO, UNICEF, other development partners through Government coordination mechanisms. UN Humanitarian Coordinator and Advisory Board of Afghanistan Humanitarian Fund involved. Coordination with humanitarian organizations for seamless humanitarian-development support.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Fast-track emergency procurement prioritized. Direct contracting with Sehatmandi Service Providers for medical services using established unit rates. Framework agreements with international agencies (UNICEF, WHO, UN agencies) for medicines, medical supplies, equipment. Direct contracting/limited competition with identified manufacturers. Increased thresholds: goods shopping to US$200,000 (from US$50,000), national procurement to US$500,000 (from US$200,000). Post-review for emergency procurement relief phases. Shorter bidding times, no bid security, advance payments, direct payments applied case-by-case. All procurement by GCMU-MOPH/Sehatmandi. High-value procurement above MOPH threshold facilitated by National Procurement Authority (NPA) Facilitation Unit. Bank provides Hands-on Expanded Implementation Support (HEIS) through procurement accredited consultant. Streamlined Project Procurement Strategy for Development to be developed during implementation.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Same M&E modalities as Sehatmandi operation. High-Level Health Program Oversight Committee ensures independence of third-party monitor (TPM) agency. Committee reviews design, implementation, and reports of TPM including proposed project key indicators. Joint External Evaluations (JEEs) inform Results Framework indicators. MOPH produces quarterly reports based on agreed targets and implementation progress with performance tables against indicators. Experienced in-country World Bank team (health, operational, fiduciary specialists) provides day-to-day implementation support. Regular implementation support missions with relevant partners. Bi-annual interim financial reports to Bank. Supreme Audit Office of Afghanistan conducts annual project audits. UN agencies provide quarterly Fund Utilization Reports showing funds received and expenditure alongside progress reports. ARTF Third-Party Monitoring Agent (TPMA) reviews underlying expenditures in SOEs quarterly. ARTF-financed Anti-Corruption and Results Monitoring Action Program (ACReMAP) monitors compliance with anti-corruption guidelines. TPM and ARTF TPMA verify goods and services delivery.

identified_risks.risk_description: President sworn into second 5-year term on March 9, 2020, but primary opponent also declared himself victor without international recognition. Continued political divide adds to prevailing political uncertainty. Uncertain whether Taliban will accept peace negotiations with newly inaugurated government. Localized security incidents resumed including in Kabul. Continuation of violence may limit health workers' ability to reach insecure areas for surveillance, community awareness raising, and medical care. Service providers face pressure from anti-government elements (AGEs) with demands for more services and illicit payments, with pressures increasing over past months.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Service delivery model used under Sehatmandi has proven resilient to conflict and insecurity, with services provided nationwide including in insecure areas or areas where Government footprint is limited. MOPH issued clear directive to SPs prohibiting any form of payment to AGEs.

identified_risks.risk_description: COVID-19 expected to have negative impacts on Afghanistan's economy through trade disruptions (closure of border crossings, export corridors affecting agricultural exports to Pakistan, Iran, India, China, Middle East), reduced remittance flows (3-7% of GDP), disruption to economic activity and human capital development, further negative impacts on already-low private sector confidence. Slower economic growth could negatively impact overstretched fiscal resources for healthcare. Reduced incomes may impact access to health services in country where health expenditure dominated by out-of-pocket spending.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project activities will help address immediate and long-term impacts by preventing loss of human capital, limiting extent and duration of economic disruption, and supporting broader health-system strengthening with positive long-run economic returns.

identified_risks.risk_description: While basic health care delivery has expanded and improved, overall health system remains weak and fragmented with considerable funding and health facilities off-budget. Public health system capacity for disease outbreak response and preparedness needs strengthening. Joint External Evaluation (JEE) identified priority areas: legislation for IHR implementation, routine capacity at points of entry, strengthening surveillance capacity, improving emergency response operations, improving risk communication.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project supports strengthening of BPHS and EPHS to incorporate health security considerations. Government developed costed national action plan (US$17.5 million). Project leverages proven implementation and fiduciary oversight arrangements from Sehatmandi Project. Partnerships with international partners, especially specialized UN agencies (WHO, UNICEF).

identified_risks.risk_description: Key challenge is availability and price of medical equipment and supplies necessary for immediate response (PPE, Oxygen Concentrator, etc.). Global supply chain disruptions and lead times. Limited production capacity in-country for most items and spread of infection in other countries. Project success depends on conducive social behavior, but high illiteracy levels, limited media reach in certain locations, and mistrust in government among population segments could challenge behavior change (adherence to social distancing, self-quarantine), as exemplified by Afghanistan's struggle to eradicate polio.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Global PSCN (Pandemic Supply Chain Network) identified critical medical products; World Bank will work with MOPH to customize list. Essential goods and supplies procured through MOUs with UN agencies (UNICEF) engaged in direct discussions with global suppliers. Advance procurement and framework agreements with UN agencies expected to mitigate supply chain risks. Project supports advocacy and coalition building to sensitize key groups including policy makers and media, ensuring consistent communication.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Afghanistan currently lacks adequate supply of trained staff for disease surveillance and frontline responses, and necessary supplies and equipment for detecting and treating COVID-19 cases. Service providers expected to be more nimble in mobilizing resources than Government, but responses across 31 provinces could prove uneven. Limited capacity to conduct emergency procurement.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Combination of intensive capacity building efforts and partnerships with international partners, especially specialized UN agencies (WHO, UNICEF). Reliance on proven delivery model of Sehatmandi. GCMU and NPA Facilitation Unit will maintain staff with appropriate capacity dedicated to COVID-19 response. Bank provides HEIS through procurement accredited consultant. Focus on training and capacity building will enhance sustainability. Outcomes related to strengthening disease surveillance and pandemic preparedness will be sustainable products of project.

identified_risks.risk_description: Afghanistan's portfolio characterized with high fiduciary risks with allegations of fraud and corruption constantly reaching Bank and numerous cases of procedural non-compliance frequently detected. Unsubstantiated allegations occasionally made in health sector. Emergency nature of project elevates risk despite mitigation measures. Risks include: political interference in awarding contracts to media for communication campaign; incomplete record and misuse of goods/assets/inventory at health facilities; overdue unacquitted cash advances, incomplete documentation, use of cash for ineligible expenditure; no established procedure for recruitment and compensation of community health workers.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project adopts enhanced fiduciary framework introduced to entire Afghanistan portfolio under Anti-Corruption and Results Monitoring Action Program (ACReMAP). SOE-based disbursement combined with ex-ante sample-based review of expenditure documents. HEIS and stepped-up post reviews in procurement. Deployment of ARTF TPMA for physical performance monitoring and fiduciary oversight. Ex-ante agreement with Bank on: criteria for selection of media houses, ceiling for contract values, use of UN agencies for communication campaign. Service providers made responsible to maintain detailed records of assets/inventory and cash advances. MOPH notification of community health workers' qualification criteria, compensation, recruitment process. MOF Treasury Department appoints focal point to ensure payment processing within three working days. Bi-annual interim financial reports to Bank. Supreme Audit Office conducts annual audits. UN agencies provide quarterly Fund Utilization Reports. ARTF TPMA reviews SOE expenditures quarterly. ACReMAP monitors anti-corruption compliance. TPM and ARTF TPMA verify goods and services delivery.

identified_risks.risk_description: Environmental risks considered High: occupational health and safety issues related to testing and handling of supplies and possibility they are not adequately used by laboratory technicians and medical crews; environmental pollution and community health and safety issues related to handling, transportation and disposal of healthcare waste. Social risks considered High: marginalized and vulnerable social groups unable to access facilities and services could undermine project objectives. Risk that services not provided equitably based on need.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: MOPH will update existing Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) prepared for Sehatmandi Project, applying international best practices in COVID-19 diagnostic testing, handling medical supplies, disposing of generated wastes. ESMF will incorporate updated Health Care Waste Management Plan. Until updated ESMF approved, project will apply existing ESMF in conjunction with WHO standards on COVID-19 response. Relevant parts of WHO COVID-19 quarantine guidelines and biosafety guidelines reviewed while updating ESMF. ESCP commits to provision of services and supplies based on urgency of need, in line with latest data on case prevalence. Draft Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) prepared to guide MOPH in early interactions with wide range of citizens (including most vulnerable) regarding basic health precautions and emergency measures. SEP to be revised within one month of project approval. SEP includes fully elaborated Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) for addressing concerns and grievances.

identified_risks.risk_description: Need for effective engagement with wide range of stakeholders including Government, UN agencies, WHO, humanitarian partners, media, community leaders, and vulnerable populations. Risk of inadequate stakeholder coordination or engagement.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Draft Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) prepared with preliminary stakeholder mapping to guide MOPH in early interactions with citizens regarding health precautions and emergency measures. SEP to be revised within one month of project approval. Project coordinates closely with Government (MOPH and MOF), UN agencies, WHO, UNICEF, humanitarian organizations through Government coordination mechanisms. UN Humanitarian Coordinator and Advisory Board of Afghanistan Humanitarian Fund involved. Project ensures coordination with humanitarian organizations for seamless humanitarian-development support.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (Relevant)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (Relevant)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (Not Currently Relevant)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (Not Currently Relevant)
Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (Not Currently Relevant)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Draft Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) includes fully elaborated Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) for addressing concerns and grievances raised by project-affected communities and individuals. Communities and individuals who believe they are adversely affected may submit complaints to project-level grievance redress mechanisms or World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS). Project-affected communities and individuals may submit complaints to World Bank's independent Inspection Panel. Information available at http://www.worldbank.org/en/projects-operations/products-and-services/grievance-redress-service and www.inspectionpanel.org
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Not explicitly detailed in PAD, but project includes support for vulnerable communities and marginalized groups through mitigation of social impacts component and commitment to provision of services based on urgency of need
vulnerable_groups: Elderly or those with existing health conditions
Populations in insecure areas with limited Government footprint
Communities affected by drought-induced displacement (nearly 300,000 individuals)
Poor and poverty-affected populations (55% basic needs poverty rate)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity considerations. Service delivery model proven resilient to conflict and insecurity, with services provided nationwide including in insecure areas where Government footprint is limited. Project addresses challenge of reaching insecure areas for surveillance, community awareness raising, and medical care despite ongoing violence. Community mobilization through existing community institutions (CDCs, health shuras, religious and tribal leaders) designed to work in conflict context. Project recognizes service providers face pressure from anti-government elements (AGEs) with demands for illicit payments and MOPH issued clear directive prohibiting such payments. Learning agenda includes how to engage in social distancing and risk communication in FCV context using mixed method approach (quantitative and qualitative methodologies).
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan designated as fragile state with multiple fragility factors: (1) Ongoing armed conflict with civilian casualties exceeding 10,000 in 2019 (3,403 killed, 6,989 injured); (2) Political uncertainty - Presidential elections results announced only in February 2020, primary opponent declared himself victor without international recognition, uncertain Taliban peace negotiations; (3) Displacement crisis - conflict-induced IDPs increased from 369,700 (2018) to over 400,000 (2019), drought-induced displacement reached nearly 300,000; (4) Refugee returns - 505,000 refugees returned in 2019, mainly from Iran; (5) International support uncertainty - current pledges due to expire December 2020; (6) Weak institutional capacity - health system fragmented with considerable off-budget funding and facilities; (7) High poverty - 55% basic needs poverty rate (2016/17), expected to have worsened; (8) Low human capital - Human Capital Index of 0.39, ranking 133 of 157 countries; (9) Weak private sector confidence constrained by political uncertainties; (10) Cross-border vulnerabilities - porous borders with Iran (Herat Province) and China with frequent travel, 2,000 Afghan students in China including 50 in Wuhan epicenter.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project recognizes security risks from ongoing conflict limiting health workers' ability to reach insecure areas. Service providers face pressure from anti-government elements (AGEs) with demands for more services and illicit payments, with pressures increasing. Localized security incidents resumed including in Kabul. Project mitigates through proven resilient service delivery model that has functioned in insecure areas. MOPH directive prohibits illicit payments to AGEs. Project includes support for community mobilization through trusted local institutions (CDCs, health shuras, religious and tribal leaders) that can function in security-constrained environment. Coordination with humanitarian organizations for seamless humanitarian-development support in fragile mechanisms.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Afghanistan experiencing significant displacement and refugee movements relevant to COVID-19 response: (1) Conflict-induced internally displaced people (IDPs) increased from 369,700 in 2018 to over 400,000 in 2019; (2) Drought-induced displacement reached record levels of nearly 300,000 individuals; (3) About 505,000 refugees returned to Afghanistan in 2019, mainly from Iran; (4) Cross-border movement - frequent travel to/from Iran and China with porous borders, particularly Herat Province bordering Iran with large and rapidly evolving COVID-19 outbreak; (5) Project scope nationwide covering all 34 provinces with initial priority in high-risk border provinces (Herat) and urban areas; (6) Project includes mitigation of social impacts component addressing negative externalities on vulnerable communities; (7) Project supports mental health and psychosocial services for vulnerable communities; (8) Commitment to provision of services and supplies based on urgency of need in line with case prevalence data; (9) Vulnerable populations including IDPs and returnees identified as beneficiaries requiring equitable access to services
